data.title: HubSpot: Buying The 'Death Of Software' Narrative Hand Over Fist (Upgrade)
data.description:
I have been looking deeply in the software sector amidst surging fears that generative AI may disrupt the space. HubSpot (HUBS) is a former growth darling, but its premium multiple has all but vanished. Amidst the chaos, the company continues to post solid top-line growth alongside significant progress on profitability. The valuation has fallen to a point at which stellar returns are possible even without a return to its former glory. I am upgrading the stock to a "Strong Buy" as beaten-down software is my top overarching theme beginning the new year.

HUBS Stock Price

I last covered HUBS in 2022, where I gave it a bullish rating. The stock has massively underperformed the market since, as it has failed to live up to the overly optimistic growth trajectory.

The re-rating downward of software stocks has created great fear as well as a buying opportunity.

HUBS Stock Key Metrics

HUBS is an enterprise software company known for its customer relationship management (‘CRM’) products. Whereas Salesforce (CRM) dominates among larger enterprises, HUBS has found a successful position servicing the small-to-medium enterprise segment. Like CRM, HUBS has integrated generative AI into its product suite. That appears not to be enough to quell concerns that it might be a victim of generative AI.

In its most recent quarter, HUBS generated 21% YoY revenue growth to $810 million, crushing guidance of between $785.0 million and $787.0 million. The company generated $161 million of non-GAAP operating profits, slightly exceeding guidance of between $156.0 million and $157.0 million.

I note that the company has made steady progress on GAAP profitability, generating its first quarter of positive GAAP operating income in the quarter.

HUBS ended the quarter with $1.7 billion of cash versus no debt. The company spent $375.0 million of cash to repurchase stock in the quarter, which is quite aggressive given the $161 million in non-GAAP operating income.

Looking ahead, management guided for the company to see around 18% YoY revenue growth to $830 million, marking some deceleration from the third quarter.

On the conference call, management called generative AI a clear opportunity, in part due to the change in search behavior. With AI overviews gaining more and more market share, the company has found success in its Loop product, which helps customers “show up in AI-generated answers.” Management was directly asked if they could return to 20% growth, to which they answered that they “believe we can grow faster than where we are today, and we are focused on doing it in a durable, disciplined way.” I would not be surprised if AI agents help accelerate the growth rates at some point in the future.

Is HUBS Stock a Buy, Sell, or Hold?

At recent prices, HUBS traded at around 5.4x forward sales. Consensus estimates call for the company to sustain around 14% to 16% top-line growth for many years.

That valuation multiple represents an in-line to slight premium to CRM. HUBS previously traded at a large relative and absolute premium in the past, with its 5-year average valuation at around 13x price to sales. I do not foresee a quick return to those levels, especially with CRM trading where it is today, as its faster relative growth is arguably more than offset by CRM’s far higher GAAP margins. Even so, this is a report about HUBS and not CRM.

Management has targeted up to 22% non-GAAP operating margins by 2027 and 25% over the long term, but I view these targets to be too conservative. The company’s gross margins stand at over 80%, and I would expect operating leverage to be quite significant if and when management decides to prioritize profitability.

Based on what I saw in the aftermath of the 2022 crash in tech stocks, I would not be surprised if that focus on profitability were to happen sooner rather than later. With HUBS trading at bargain basement multiples and growth possibly set to face some headwinds, it would make sense for the company to drive more of its excitement from margin expansion. Based on 25% GAAP net margins over the long term, the stock trades at around 22x long-term earnings. That looks like a cheap multiple relative to the 16% forward top-line growth rate. I typically assume that these names can achieve at least 30% GAAP net margins over the long term. I base that on the higher margins seen at mature names like Adobe (ADBE) and Microsoft (MSFT). In the case of HUBS, this target looks reasonable, especially with the company on the cusp of sustainable GAAP operating profitability. That would place the stock at around 18x long-term earnings, which would be a hair above a 1x price-to-earnings growth ratio (‘PEG ratio’). I see HUBS re-rating to a 1.5x PEG ratio at the very least, equating to around 7.2x sales or a $500 stock price over the next 12 months. The main catalyst would be the aforementioned projected margin expansion, continued execution on its share repurchase program, and any sentiment shift with regard to its business model quality. I still view HUBS to be the kind of sticky software vendor that customers are unlikely to switch away from, and thus would not rule out the stock eventually reaching more premium valuations in the 10x to 12x sales range (equating to 33x to 40x long-term earnings). HUBS is offering considerable upside to 7.2x sales given what I view to be more than manageable risk.

The key question, of course, is whether the company is at risk due to generative AI allowing its customers to simply “vibe code” an in-house product. CTO and co-founder Dharmesh Shah made a post on X addressing this very topic, noting that they themselves have not replaced any of their enterprise software subscriptions, as it makes far more sense to buy instead of build. I agree with that take - at the end of the day, I find it highly unlikely that HUBS’s customers would be able to (or desire to) develop a competitive product and maintain it at an acceptable cost.

I find it interesting that the market, primarily non-software-focused individuals, has somehow come to the collective conclusion that generative AI will disrupt enterprise software even if we have yet to see enterprise software companies themselves vibe code replacement products in-house. It is possible that one consequence of generative AI is that it has empowered the individual in ways not seen before, but perhaps over-extrapolation of that empowerment has created the opportunity we see in HUBS today.

HUBS Stock Risks

It is possible that generative AI really does lead to disruption. Perhaps even if vibe coding does not prove to be the disruptive force that the market believes it is, CRM might be able to use generative AI to improve its competitive positioning. It is possible that management engages in expensive M&A that destroys shareholder value. It is possible that the company is unable to realize operating leverage (though this seems unlikely given the promises of efficiency from AI).

HUBS Stock Conclusion

HUBS might see growth rates move lower before they go higher, if ever, as there probably will be some potential headwinds from generative AI. That said, I think the magnitude of the potential slowdown is being priced in too aggressively to the downside. The company maintains a net cash balance sheet and looks ready to enter positive GAAP operating profitability. The stock looks too cheap here, and I expect a re-rating as management executes on margin expansion. HUBS is the kind of stock I look for in the Best of Breed Growth Stocks Portfolio. HUBS is a "Strong Buy."

data.title: Huntington Bancshares: Well-Positioned Into Q1 Earnings
data.description:
Shares of Huntington Bancshares (HBAN) have been a solid performer over the past year, gaining just under 10%. 2025 was a transformative year for the regional bank, which did two acquisitions as it has sought to scale up, expand into higher-growth markets like Texas, and deepen its exposure to higher-margin businesses like wealth management. This strategic direction, combined with its conservative management team, has made me a long-term bull on HBAN. I last covered HBAN in October, rating the stock a “Buy,” and since then, shares have gained 4%, in line with the market. With HBAN now integrating its acquisitions, now is a good time to preview Q1 earnings and see if the stock remains attractive.

HBAN Transformed via M&A in 2025

Before looking forward to Q1 earnings, it is worth taking a moment to recap what was a very eventful year in 2025. First, in July, Huntington announced it was acquiring Veritex to scale up its Texas operation. This was really a bolt-on transaction at $1.9 billion in stock. It increases the size of HBAN by about 6%. Huntington received regulatory approval and closed the deal in October. HBAN is now in the process of integrating Veritex, and its Q4 financials will be the first quarter to include a contribution from this entity.

Then, surprisingly, in October, Huntington announced a second deal, accelerating its growth ambitions, with the purchase of Cadence Bank (CADE). This purchase is consistent with Huntington’s strategy to expand in the South with a significant presence in Texas and Mississippi as well as presences in other key Sun Belt metros, like Nashville, Atlanta, Tampa, and Orlando. With this deal, HBAN will operate in 21 states and half of America’s top 25 metros. Increasingly, HBAN is turning from a regional to a super-regional bank, a strategy I am supportive of given the need to increase scale and efficiency to compete with the largest institutions.

This deal is larger, with a $7.4 billion equity price, and like Veritex, the transaction is entirely in stock. Given Cadence’s larger size, there is definitely more integration risk, though it is manageable. This deal will add about $53 billion in assets, increasing the size of HBAN by just under 25% and making it one of the ten largest banks in the nation. Still, it is a fraction of America’s largest banks that have over $2 trillion in assets, and accordingly I do not expect much antitrust risk to the completion of the deal, especially as most of Cadence’s assets are not in HBAN’s legacy Midwest markets. The deal is ultimately expected to boost HBAN earnings power by about 10%, though there will be limited in-year impact in 2026. Shareholders have approved the deal, and HBAN expects to close it on Feb. 1. As a result, Q1 earnings are likely to be messy, given integration and closing costs.

HBAN is Poised for Ongoing Growth Now

As you can see below, HBAN’s network of branches is significantly larger than it previously was. Historically, it operated in Michigan, Ohio, and other Midwest markets. Going forward, it now has a significant presence across much of the Sun Belt, giving HBAN a profile more similar to a bank like Fifth Third (FITB). These Sun Belt markets are generally faster-growing and rich in deposits and wealth management opportunities. Additionally, with many retirees being “snowbirds” who spend much or all of their time in the Southeast, this footprint makes it easier for HBAN to meet all of its clients’ needs, especially as they reach peak wealth accumulation.

As you can see below, HBAN has historically been able to grow more quickly than peers, and loan growth has been nearly 5% over the past five years. Scaling up its presence in Chicago has facilitated this growth, but this pace is all the more impressive considering HBAN has historically been focused on the slower-growth portion of the country. With the go-forward HBAN operating across more growth markets, the ability to sustain this loan growth is heightened.

HBAN is Likely to Beat Q4 Earnings

Now, turning to earnings, Huntington is scheduled to report Q4 earnings on July 22, and analysts currently expect the bank to earn $0.38 on an adjusted basis and $0.32 on a GAAP basis. I would note that closing an acquisition during a quarter, particularly in the banking sector, can create significant accounting noise as all assets and liabilities are marked to market. Given Veritex’s comparatively small size, the EPS impact should not be too meaningful, but I do see scope for volatility in GAAP EPS, though any impact will be excluded from adjusted EPS, which will be the market’s focus. HBAN expects an $8 million impact from Veritex purchase accounting.

Huntington has a strong record of beating consensus, and it has not missed earnings since 2021. The company has also effectively talked down consensus over appearances at several conferences in late Q4, and with 14 analyst cuts over the past 90 days, HBAN has been able to set the consensus more conservative, which should make it easier to report an earnings beat next week.

Speaking at a Goldman Sachs conference in December, Huntington provided an intra-quarter update. As you can see below, growth continued to be solid in Q4. Loans were up nearly $3 billion through two months, while deposits were also up $1.3 billion. Oftentimes, loan growth slows in the latter part of December (given vacations around the holidays), while cash can seasonally build, boosting deposits. As such, I would not be surprised to see these growth rates converge a bit when actual results are announced.

In fact, given the strong performance QTD, HBAN provided insight as to how results are tracking relative to guidance. Both loans and deposits are tracking at the high end of the range, and as a result, net interest income should come in stronger than expected. Critically, with the focus on loan quality having risen amid challenges in private credit, the company still sees net charge-offs at the midpoint of its range at a very healthy mid-20bp level. All of this suggests a modest beat; I expect HBAN to earn $0.38-$0.42 when it reports Q4 results.

HBAN Has Further Upside; Shares Are a "Buy"

Alongside results, there will be significant focus on the company’s outlook as well as its capital allocation policy. Announcing two deals in ~three months is fairly aggressive, and I want to see HBAN focus primarily on integrating Cadence and continuing to grow organically. Investors will want to be sure that it is not doing too much too quickly. To that end, in December, management signaled there was no interest in further expanding its geographic footprint, and any deals would be in its existing territory to build more scale.

I expect HBAN to spend the next six months integrating Cadence and building capital to support organic growth. I am targeting mid-single-digit deposit and loan growth in 2026 with charge-offs that should be similar to 2025. As a result, I am targeting about $1.67-$1.77 of 2026 earnings. This assumes no share repurchases, as I expect HBAN to retain earnings to support organic growth, though investors have a secure 3.4% dividend yield. Absent M&A, I see scope for buybacks in 2027, but I would not be surprised to see HBAN do more bolt-on dealmaking before year-end and delay buybacks.

I view Huntington Bancshares as a secular winner within the regional banking sector. It has enjoyed solid organic growth while maintaining strong loan quality, enabling consistent earnings beats. In 2025, the company undertook significant M&A to transform its footprint, and we should start to see the benefits this year, though I expect even more benefits to appear in 2027 as it completes integration work and focuses on accelerating growth in new, attractive Sun Belt markets. I view 12x earnings as appropriate, given its leading growth profile, pointing to upside past $20. I view shares as a "Buy" into earnings.

data.title: Ruffer LLP Q4 2025 Investment Review
data.description:
It’s more than 30 years since I wrote my first Investment Review, delivered in paper form, courtesy of the Royal Mail, and to interested parties at a postage cost of 22p. Thirty-one years on, both the price of postage and the level of courtesy have changed considerably.

It was a forward-looking review, which is unsurprising, given that Ruffer at the time had no history – we were a business trundling slowly down the runway in a light plane on a long journey. As I approach retirement, this will be my last formal review – the pen may be silent, but the thought processes will continue. I shall continue to meet with my former colleagues and will thereby find myself ensconced in the happiest of circumstances, having the power of articulation but without any responsibilities.

The history and theory of investing is as old as the history of warfare, and one might reasonably think that all that could be said of each of them has been already articulated. The greatest ‘insight’ to become a reality in the 1980s was Jack Bogle’s. It was a simple one: if you are happy with average performance, then why not enjoy an above-average performance by investing in a low-cost index-hugging mutual fund? This simple idea – which transcended any insight as to the nature of an investment – has changed the face of the investing community. It has conquered the world by its monopoly, shielded from greedy competitors by its negligible price.

In modesty, I have to concede that an idea which now controls some 40% of the world’s equity and fixed interest markets is better than my contribution to the efficacy of investing. Ruffer Investment Management’s first glossy brochure stated that our investment strategy consisted of running risk portfolios which were designed not to lose money in any calendar year. Even accountants know that risk amplifies risk as well as reward, thus only riskless assets can claim to be free of the poison of capital reduction. And here was Ruffer, with no track record, saying you can have your cake and eat it.

The universal response: Jonathan Ruffer’s a lovely guy (I can only agree with that assessment), and what he’s offering, if achieved, would be wonderful. But something that is too good to be true is, er, well…not true. That was in 1994. In the event, it took until 2018 for our first failure to meet our objectives, although some years effectively produced nothing. The going has been more volatile since, with 2023 falling far short, but our long-term track record still puts to shame the riskless alternative of cash in the bank.

We don’t know ahead of time which opportunities are going to be the actual winners and which the losers – the gold and the fool’s gold are intertwined in an inextricable embrace.

In 2023, we found ourselves accused of not doing what we claimed to do – almost as if ‘cake and eat it’ was something that could be achieved faultlessly. Despite the head wound I felt from letting clients down that year, it was hard to escape the feeling that this way of investing has come of age. When I began, our investment disciplines were as much intuitive as rigorous. Today, they are incomparably better – so much so that I find myself (against all the evidence) lost for words. It has been a pleasure to report meeting our objectives strongly in 2025, a year which has increased the long-term track record of Ruffer.

How risky is the strategy? At one level, we concede that our aim not to lose money in any given year is a parallel claim to most professional investors’ ambition to ‘beat the indices.’ That ambition is accepted as an aspiration, and even the very best will achieve it only intermittently. The ‘beat the indices’ target has a defect in that, in the ebb and flow of performance, at certain intervals the bad investment professional will look rather good, and (of course) vice versa. So, for an investor adequately to gauge whether their decision to hire a manager was a good one or not requires a commitment of an unnervingly long duration. Ours is, by contrast, an absolute target, judged annually. We can safely be sacked much sooner than our index-related competitors.

What, then, are the characteristics of what we do? The only reason it works is because investments routinely, regularly, become either too cheap or too expensive. While only riskless commitments guarantee no loss, all of us can see that the imperfections of a real-live market allow for an imbalance of risk and reward, some favourable, some unfavourable, in specific investment opportunities. There is, of course, always an elephant in the room. We don’t know ahead of time which opportunities are going to be the actual winners, and which the losers – the gold and the fool’s gold are intertwined in an inextricable embrace. While each individual opportunity might not give up its secrets as to future performance, it is often possible to juxtapose two (or maybe more), which removes the unknowable risks and offers each-way upside. The ideal might, for instance, be a set of investments which do well if the oil price goes up, and a different set which will create outperformance if it goes down. In this simple example, the result is to create a different risk (eg political risk), which must then be considered separately. As a result, an investor in the Ruffer strategy is diversified away from market risk and left with the risk attached to our judgements of juxtaposing assets and portfolio construction. That is the skill we employ. While the strategy is simple, the execution is often complex.

What we took for wisdom 18 months ago has turned out to be so comprehensively wrong that my generation really don’t know what to think and hum the tune from 'My Fair Lady,' 'Just you wait, 'enry 'iggins, just you wait!'

Today we see a world where the thoughtless control the show and are making hay. A world where there is a generational battle going on, where the greybeards look at that world with incredulity. What we took for wisdom 18 months ago has turned out to be so comprehensively wrong that my generation really don’t know what to think, and hum the tune from ‘My Fair Lady’, ‘Just you wait, ’enry ’iggins, just you wait!’ Unlike my daughter, who is an academic neuroscientist, I haven’t quite got to understand how the brain works. But I find one quirk of the brain vividly fascinating: when humans think that some futurity is absolutely certain to happen, that thought cannot be disconnected from a second one – that the future event will happen very soon.

It is well understood that the generation now known as Boomers – those born before 1965 – have a larger share of the asset gravy than the generations which have followed. Today, to be in the top 1% of UK earners by income, your take-home pay would be around £90,000 per year; to be in the top 1% by assets, you need to own nearly £4 million, a sum which would take the 1% incomer over 44 years to accumulate if they spent not a penny in the meantime. This means that, for the first time since Magna Carta, people are not as well off as their parents, especially in the realm of housing. A successful professional, mid-forties, can see that the capital sum required to buy the house they grew up in is multiples more than they can afford, whether in cash or by borrowing. Suppose such a person has capital of £400,000, and the house they want is £1.8 million. There is no point going to a conventional fund manager who is pleased with a double-digit gain. What good is £440,000 when the purchase of a family house is required? But, if one is prepared to make an educated and well-informed foray into crypto, or AI, and achieve 25% a year, then it will have tripled in five years – and many of their contemporaries seem to have been doing this successfully for ten years or more. The only golden rule in this approach to investment: 'Never look down!' There will be sickening crashes and, if you try to analyse why, you will of course be strongly tempted to lose your nerve, and cash in. A lot of people made that ‘mistake’ in 2018, quite a lot in 2020, rather fewer on Liberation Day (6 April 2025), and today the warriors, as a cadre, will not make that ‘mistake’ again. This market will go on incredulifying the greybeards until it doesn’t. There’s a great line in JK Galbraith’s book on the 1929 crash, when he describes an eminent economist as ‘standing his ground’ before adding ‘but unfortunately the ground gave way under him.’

This moment is inevitable, but it need not be at all soon. If it is long delayed, it will represent salvation to those who put the motorised engine onto their monetary barge and achieved what was necessary. The question in my mind is what is its aftermath? If the uncertainties and dislocations can be managed, then, yes, there will be losers, many of them, and there will be many winners, too. But if it undermines the very fabric of the financial system, as was the case in 1873 and 1929, then the catalyst can be a mere water pistol and not the true weapon of war. In that case, the participants – the economy, the families going about their business, the high hopes of the young, the conservatism of the old – will find that the ground on which they depend gives way. The longer animal spirits control the market, the greater the ultimate confusion.

Past performance is not a guide to future performance. The value of the shares and the income from them can go down as well as up and you may not get back the full amount originally invested. The value of overseas investments will be influenced by the rate of exchange.

The views expressed in this article are not intended as an offer or solicitation for the purchase or sale of any investment or financial instrument, including interests in any of Ruffer’s funds. The information contained in the article is fact based and does not constitute investment research, investment advice or a personal recommendation, and should not be used as the basis for any investment decision. References to specific securities are included for the purposes of illustration only and should not be construed as a recommendation to buy or sell these securities. This article does not take account of any potential investor’s investment objectives, particular needs or financial situation. This article reflects Ruffer’s opinions at the date of publication only, the opinions are subject to change without notice and Ruffer shall bear no responsibility for the opinions offered. Read the full disclaimer

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: TSPY Vs. SPYI: Why This 0DTE Fund Might Be Even Better Than SPYI
data.description:
Over the last couple of years, the competition among ETF providers to deliver high-yielding ETFs has led to numerous innovative income products. NEOS was one of the first fund providers to really change the covered call ETF space with its flexible out-of-the-money ("OTM") call selling and focus on tax-efficient distributions. As a result, funds like the Neos S&P 500 High Income ETF (SPYI) have garnered over $7B in assets under management ("AUM") - eclipsing AUM levels of formerly larger and more established funds like the Global X S&P 500 Covered Call ETF (XYLD).

But as fund providers continue to find different angles, SPYI has started to see its standing as one of, if not the best, S&P 500 covered call ETFs being challenged. The TappAlpha SPY Growth & Daily Income ETF (TSPY) is one of those new funds, which, despite its limited track record both as a fund and fund provider, has been delivering phenomenal performances that may be more preferable to SPYI's. It is a fund that offers more yield and higher S&P 500 upside capture, with its biggest drawback compared to SPYI being its weaker downside protection. For most income investors, however, this weaker downside protection is not necessarily a huge issue, especially given TSPY's higher upside potential. While I wouldn't necessarily sell SPYI for TSPY, it may make sense now to treat TSPY as more of a core covered call income holding. I still maintain a Buy rating on SPYI due to its consistent performance and rate TSPY as a Strong Buy.

SPYI's Strengths

What has made SPYI especially attractive over the last couple of years is how it has not only consistently generated 10%+ annual yields but also delivered price appreciation and positive total returns.

The fund has achieved this by selling monthly OTM SPX covered calls and, occasionally, credit spreads. Instead of selling calls at a fixed percentage strike price every month like funds such as XYLD, SPYI chooses its strike price to maximize growth in its underlying S&P 500 constituent holdings while targeting 12% of its NAV annualized in option premium earnings. SPYI's current trades at the time of writing include two call options expiring February 20, 2026. One has a strike price of 6985 and the other at 7055. For context, the S&P 500 is currently at 6966. The premiums generated represent approximately 1% of SPYI's net assets, which puts the fund on track to generate a 12% yield from option premiums.

Now, what has made SPYI even more attractive has been its focus on delivering tax-efficient returns. It is a common misconception that SPYI's distribution yields are too aggressive and it is simply returning your capital due to its large return of capital ("ROC") distributions. As indicated by SPYI's historical trades, however, nothing suggests that the fund is unable to cover distributions. What should be understood about SPYI's ROC is that it's a result of the fund's tax-loss harvesting strategies and done on purpose to create more "tax-efficient" distributions. ROC is a distribution classification where the payout is not immediately taxable and instead reduces your cost basis. You will then be taxed at capital gains rates when your cost basis reaches zero or when you sell your asset, in which your profit will be the sell price minus your adjusted cost basis. SPYI's distributions have averaged 82.04% ROC since its inception.

Part of the reason SPYI has also been tax-efficient is because it sells index options. Option premiums from index options are subject to 60% taxation as long-term capital gains and 40% as short-term rather than 100% short-term under section 1256 rules. This is much better compared to funds like the JPMorgan Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPI), which generate 100% ordinary income using swaps. Most investors are better off taking ROC or capital gains distributions tax-wise, but please do consult a professional tax advisor for your specific situation.

The Rise of TSPY

TSPY has many similar characteristics to SPYI. The fund is also anticipated to be tax-efficient like SPYI, as it sells 1256 index options and makes use of tax-loss harvesting strategies to generate ROC distributions. Just like SPYI, TSPY has also implemented the capability to be more flexible with its trading strategies and implement tools like call spreads. What makes TSPY potentially even better than SPYI, however, is it uses 0 days-to-expiry ("0DTE") options that have resulted in higher distribution payouts and upside capture. TSPY's yield has been noticeably higher, with it hovering around the 14% range compared to SPYI's 12%. But at the same time, TSPY's price returns have been comparable, and past total returns have indicated that TSPY does a better job of capturing gains in the S&P 500 than SPYI.

The reason TSPY has been able to accomplish this feat is because 0DTE options generally give the option sellers more opportunity to make profitable trades without sacrificing upside. Part of this is due to the fact that TSPY closes its trades by the end of the day and has overnight exposure to its underlying holdings. Studies have shown that overnight returns do often surpass intraday returns, which is why 0DTE funds like TSPY have actually been able to keep up with the underlying index in total returns.

Of course, this can go the other way as well. Just as how overnight returns can suddenly spike up, they can also fall significantly, and that is why 0DTE funds have essentially offered negligible downside protection. You can really see this when you compare TSPY with SPY and SPYI during April 2025. TSPY's options always expired by the end of the day, while SPYI's monthly options were still intact, and the value on the options can still help offset any losses in the underlying.

To visualize 0DTE's weaker downside protection and increased upside capture and see how the two funds may compare in different markets, we can take a look at both funds' trades. I already mentioned that SPYI has one call option with a strike at 6985 and another at 7055, both expiring February 20, 2026. For TSPY, I have collected its trade data for the week of January 5th, 2026.

Additionally, I have also provided open and close prices of the S&P 500 for the same time period below alongside total returns for TSPY, SPYI, and SPY:

The story that these tables and charts tell us is that on January 5th, TSPY made a profitable trade. Their option position expired worthless by the end of the day, and TSPY captured the full overnight growth in the SPX going to January 6th, plus the premiums. This is why TSPY actually outperformed SPY on January 6th. On the 6th, however, the SPX experienced significant growth that led to TSPY having to cash settle its position and take a loss. The money lost on this trade would show up the next day, where TSPY ultimately returned lower than SPY as the SPX also closed lower. Fortunately for TSPY, the next few days would be better, as the fund's trades were all profitable and gave the fund an opportunity to experience all the gains in the underlying. SPYI, on the other hand, sold monthly options. Being short these options makes them a liability on SPYI's balance sheets. As the underlying SPX moves closer toward or further away from SPYI's option strike prices, the liability of these options may increase or decrease, respectively, and it is reflected in the relatively more muted total returns.

While neither TSPY nor SPYI will have the exact same option trades every day or month, they will (outside extenuating circumstances) trade options daily and monthly. Closing option positions daily will mean that TSPY experiences essentially "naked" exposure to the underlying S&P 500. As you can see above, if the S&P 500 rises too suddenly during the day, it may force TSPY to close out its position and cap its upside. During the night, however, TSPY will experience the full changes from the previous close to the new day's open. SPYI, on the other hand, will experience more muted returns both up and down, as the value of its options always responds to price movements and affects SPYI's performance.

With this understanding, you can have a solid image of how TSPY's and SPYI's performances will compare against each other and the S&P 500 in various market circumstances, even without knowing the actual trades. In downward trending markets, TSPY will generally experience the full downside minus any premiums earned, while SPYI's options position can help offset unrealized losses. In upward-trending markets, both funds are capped by their options' strikes, but TSPY will be better off if the overnight returns are positive, as they experience full underlying exposure.

Why You Might Prefer TSPY

For most income investors, it is arguable that this extra volatility in TSPY compared to SPYI is well worth it. First, consider that both TSPY and SPYI are funds that will sell covered calls on a regular basis. Selling covered calls regularly is a strategy that will cap your upside in your underlying assets, and this upside cap has been regarded as the greatest risk in investing in these covered call ETFs. Take XYLD, a fund that sells monthly SPX covered calls with strike prices at-the-money ("ATM"), for example. XYLD is a fund that performed admirably in 2022 with total returns that outpaced and was less volatile than the S&P 500. In the bull markets of 2024 and 2025, however, the S&P 500 ultimately ended up doubling XYLD's returns as the fund capped its upside. At the same time, you should also note XYLD's NAV erosion, where its price returns have remained at nearly negative 20% since 2022.

No investor wants to see their investment on a constant decline, so the trend had shifted toward how a covered call fund might be able to capture more upside while still maintaining high yields. And so far, TSPY has demonstrated the most capability in achieving this with one of the highest total return levels relative to the S&P 500 and a yield averaging 13-14% since its inception.

As mentioned in my previous article on the NEOS S&P 500 Hedged Equity Income ETF (SPYH), income investors must also make a serious consideration of how important downside protection really is. Generally, the cases where you actually need real downside protection in your capital are very specific, where numbers are known. For example, you might have a loan backed by your assets, and your portfolio is only allowed to drop 10%. Otherwise, it becomes mostly just a peace of mind thing.

When it comes to comparing SPYI and TSPY, you'll find that SPYI's lower volatility does not ultimately change things that much at the end of the day. Since August 15, 2024, TSPY's rolling 1-month beta peaked at 1.2, while SPYI's was around 0.9, with the S&P 500 as the underlying asset. Both funds hold the S&P 500's constituents as their underlying holdings and will experience largely the same drops in value.

TSPY's drop may be slightly higher, while SPYI's may be slightly less. To put things in perspective, a 30% drop in SPY may result in a 36% drop in TSPY versus a 27% drop in SPYI. This lower volatility, however, does not necessarily justify taking lower upsides and dividend payouts. Most income investors are better off using TSPY's higher distributions to reinvest during downturns and grow their income stream faster, rather than relying on SPYI's extra downside protection.

And the discussion of volatility and TSPY's upside capture should be put in the broader market context. In 2026, pressing issues on hand already include Trump's strike on Venezuela, U.S.-Iran geopolitical tensions, and U.S. isolationist policies that have withdrawn the U.S. from many international organizations. Internally, judicial probes into Fed Chair Jerome Powell and a slowdown in earnings by AI-related companies point to 2026 being a highly volatile year. Major geopolitical events like Trump's initial tariff announcement in April 2025 may shock global markets, but they may just as equally prop up the market. Both the State Street Energy Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLE) and State Street Industrial Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLI) have already returned 3.65% and 5.19% year-to-date, following the Venezuela strike, as U.S. control over a country with one of the largest oil reserves and potential war could lead to increased economic potential for many companies within these sectors.

What this means for TSPY and SPYI is that during these times of increased volatility, when the market experiences both sudden rises and drops, SPYI will generally offer more muted returns, while TSPY will be able to capture more of the rebound that follows any market correction. Over the long term, TSPY should generally come out ahead so long as the U.S. economy and stock market do well.

Conclusion

All of this does not necessarily mean that I would sell SPYI and replace it with TSPY. The capital gains tax incursion does not make any sense, and there's always value in diversifying among fund providers. This is especially the case since SPYI has continued to be one of the best-performing S&P 500 covered call ETFs. That said, TSPY has demonstrated its worth as well, offering higher payouts and upside capture than SPYI at the cost of less downside protection. For most income investors, however, the higher upside is often worth the weaker downside protection. Neither fund is suitable as actual hedges in the first place, so the investor mentality is to understand that drops matching the S&P 500 will happen and should only serve as opportunities to reinvest at lower prices. I maintain a Strong Buy rating on TSPY and a Buy rating on SPYI.

data.title: Defensive Positioning Beneath A Calm Vol Market
data.description:
By Mandy Xu

Cross-Asset Volatility: Implied volatilities were mostly range-bound last week despite rising geopolitical risk. Equity, rates, credit, and FX volatilities all ended the week near a 1-year low (see Exhibit 1). Oil was the only notable mover, with 1M implied volatility jumping up over 8 vol pts to 36.8% (70th percentile high). As we highlighted last week, option market positioning has become increasingly bullish in oil, with outsized demand for upside calls driving an inversion in oil skew (i.e., calls are now trading at a premium to puts). This is evident in both options on the commodity (Brent/WTI) as well as USO (US Oil Fund ETF). Despite the threat of more supplies coming online from Venezuela, traders appear to be more focused on the near-term risk of potential supply disruptions stemming from the Iran unrest. Even with Trump’s escalating attacks on the Fed over the weekend, cross-asset vols appear fairly tame this morning as investors weigh the risk of Fed independence. The VIX® index is up just 1.5 pts pre-market to ~16 while FX volatility is up ~0.3 pts across the majors.

Equity Volatility: While the VIX index ended last week unch’d at 14.5%, there were notable changes underneath the index surface. S&P fixed strike vols increased modestly while investors sold calls to buy puts, leading to a meaningful steepening in put skew & convexity while the call wings came in (see Exhibit 2). These factors were enough to offset the “expected” 1.2 pt decline in the VIX index as a result of the market rally (SPX® index +1.6% wk/wk). The key takeaway from our VIX index decomposition is that defensive positioning increased meaningfully last week despite an unch’d VIX index level. The change in sentiment can also be seen in SPX 1M put skew (25D/50D ratio) which steepened to the 62nd percentile high and 1M put convexity (10D/25D ratio), which jumped from the 25th percentile low to the 72nd percentile high last week.

Correlation & Dispersion: While index vol was flat last week, single stock volatility continued to increase heading into earnings season. VIXEQSM Index gained another 2 vol pts to 36% and the spread between single stock vs. index vol widened to ~22 pts (90th percentile high; see Exhibit 3). Higher single stock vol hasn’t translated into higher index vol, however, as correlation levels have fallen to extremes. SPX 1M implied correlation, as measured by the COR1M index, fell to a near record low of 6.8% on Friday – the only time it has traded lower was a brief blip in July 2024. Stock & sector selection become more important during periods of low correlation, as the spread between winners and losers widen and potential stock-picking alpha increases.

Cross-Asset Volatility Monitor

Cross-Asset Volatility Snapshot (10Y Lookback)

How to read this chart:

The chart shows how various asset class implied volatilities are trading relative to their own history, using a 10-year lookback.

The volatilities are ranked by z-scores, which compares the current volatility level for an asset versus its own 10-year history. A z-score of +2, for example, would mean that the current volatility level is trading 2 standard deviations above its long-term average (i.e., rich), while a z-score of -2 would mean it’s trading 2 standard deviations below average (i.e., cheap).

Because each asset class volatility trades at different levels and often defined differently (e.g., lognormal vs. normal vol, price vs. bps vol), by standardizing using Z-scores, we’re able to compare different asset class volatilities on the same scale - and see at a glance if there are any divergences or dislocations in the cross-asset volatility markets

Cross-Asset Correlation Matrix (1M)

Cross-Asset Correlation Analysis

Macro Equity Volatility

VIX Index Volatility US International Cross-Asset

US Index Volatility

Disclaimers

There are important risks associated with transacting in any of the Cboe Company products discussed here. Before engaging in any transactions in those products, it is important for market participants to carefully review the disclosures and disclaimers contained at: Disclosures and Disclaimers Related to Cboe Options and Futures Products.

These products are complex and are suitable only for sophisticated market participants. In certain jurisdictions, Cboe Company products are only permitted for investment professionals, certified sophisticated investors, or high-net-worth corporations and associations. These products involve the risk of loss, which can be substantial and, depending on the type of product, can exceed the amount of money deposited in establishing the position. Market participants should put at risk only funds that they can afford to lose without affecting their lifestyle.

© 2026 Cboe Exchange, Inc. All Rights Reserved

data.title: IYH: Elevated Valuations And Slowing Growth Signal Subpar 2026 Returns
data.description:
Investment Thesis

iShares U.S. Healthcare ETF (IYH) warrants a hold rating due to concerning signals from its top healthcare holdings, including reduced sales growth, external pressures, and high valuations. These factors will contribute to underperformance for the fund overall compared to similar healthcare funds.

iShares U.S. Healthcare ETF - Overview and Compared ETFs

IYH is an ETF that seeks to capture a broad composition of U.S. holdings within the healthcare sector. With its inception in 2000, the fund has 103 holdings and $3.6B in AUM. IYH is entirely focused on the healthcare sector, with top-weighted industries of pharmaceuticals (33.7%), biotechnology (20.8%), and healthcare equipment (20.6%). I wrote previously on IYH and rated the fund a hold. For the reasons I discuss in this article, I believe the fund still warrants a hold.

For comparison purposes, other funds examined are VanEck Biotech ETF (BBH), iShares U.S. Healthcare Providers ETF (IHF), and iShares Global Healthcare ETF (IXJ). BBH captures holdings within the healthcare sector that are focused on genetics analysis, pharmaceuticals, and diagnostic equipment. IHF offers investors with a slightly different aspect of the healthcare sector by capturing health insurance, diagnostics, and treatment companies. IXJ differs from IYH in that it seeks to capture global healthcare holdings as opposed to strictly companies within the United States.

Funds Compared: Performance, Expense Ratio, and Dividend Yield

IYH has seen the best 10-year average annual return of 9.44%. By comparison, BBH has seen the worst performance with a 10-year average annual return of just 4.54%. IHF and IXJ have 10-year average annual returns of 8.13% and 8.17%, respectively. As a result, each fund has underperformed the broader market, as measured by the S&P 500 Index, over the past decade. However, for reasons discussed in this article, I believe IYH will underperform even its own historic average looking forward.

While there are multiple healthcare-focused ETFs with extremely low fees, such as Vanguard’s Healthcare ETF (VHT), the compared funds covered in this article all have similar fees. Additionally, IYH’s dividend yield is comparable with peer healthcare funds.

Expense Ratio, AUM, and Dividend Yield Comparison

IYH

BBH

IHF

IXJ

Expense Ratio

0.38%

0.35%

0.38%

0.40%

AUM

$3.59B

$381.80M

$778.98M

$4.66B

Dividend Yield TTM

1.17%

0.49%

1.01%

1.37%

Dividend Growth 5 YR CAGR

6.19%

9.49%

14.93%

6.93%

Source: Seeking Alpha, 10 Jan 26

IYH Holdings and Key Outlook Factors

Despite each fund focusing on the healthcare sector, different tracked indexes result in very different holdings mixes. This is particularly true for BBH and IHF which track biotech and healthcare providers, respectively. IYH is similar to other broad-based healthcare funds like IXJ which include Eli Lilly (LLY), Johnson & Johnson (JNJ), and AbbVie (ABBV) as top holdings.

Top 10 Holdings for IYH and Compared Healthcare ETFs

IYH – 103 holdings

BBH – 26 holdings

IHF – 62 holdings

IXJ – 114 holdings

LLY – 14.59%

AMGN – 14.86%

UNH – 22.72%

LLY – 10.60%

JNJ – 8.31%

GILD – 13.81%

CVS – 12.31%

JNJ – 6.10%

ABBV – 6.66%

VRTX – 9.23%

ELV – 10.02%

ABBV – 4.87%

UNH – 5.30%

REGN – 7.83%

HUM – 4.65%

UNH – 3.87%

MRK – 4.69%

IQV – 4.72%

CI – 4.65%

ROG – 3.67%

TMO – 3.84%

NTRA – 4.55%

VEEV – 4.53%

AZN – 3.64%

ABT – 3.67%

ARGX – 4.42%

HCA – 4.40%

MRK – 3.39%

ISRG – 3.47%

ALNY – 4.28%

CNC – 3.41%

NOVN – 3.33%

AMGN – 2.98%

INSM – 4.12%

LH – 3.28%

TMO – 2.81%

GILD – 2.52%

ONC – 3.17%

DGX – 3.08%

ABT – 2.70%

Source: Multiple, compiled by author on 10 Jan 26

IYH’s high concentration on LLY, JNJ, and ABBV will be an influential factor for the fund looking forward. Unfortunately, each of these top holdings have concerning signals that will likely weigh down IYH overall. These factors are discussed in further detail below.

Eli Lilly: GLP-1 Showing Signs of Cracking

The first concerning factor is the GLP-1 market which will greatly impact IYH’s top holding, Eli Lilly. GLP-1, or glucagon-like peptide-1, is a bodily hormone that helps regulate blood sugar levels. Eli Lilly’s GLP-1 product is tirzepatide, which is more commonly known as Zepbound and Mounjaro, used to treat weight loss and diabetes, respectively. With over 50% of Eli Lilly’s revenue coming from these two products, the company faces significant risk if these products decline in sales. The first factor that may impact sales is CVS Health’s (CVS) recent coverage change. Last year, the pharmacy chain stated that it will no longer cover Zepbound but will cover its competitor, Wegovy, made by Novo Nordisk (NVO). While the weight loss drug market continued to climb late last year, Eli Lilly only saw disappointing sales for Zepbound over the previous quarter.

Looking forward, Zepbound will continue to face external pressures impacting its sales. These include a string of lawsuits by patients who have reported stomach paralysis because of taking the product. Additionally, the agreement between the U.S. federal government, Eli Lilly, and Novo Nordisk to lower costs of GLP-1 medications will further pressure Eli Lilly. These factors are compounded by Eli Lilly’s high valuation. With a forward P/E of 44.9, the pharmaceutical company’s future growth is already captured in its share price. Therefore, LLY will weigh down IYH in 2026 and the holding will see lower growth than has been seen in the past couple of years.

Johnson & Johnson: Low Growth and Dividend Payout will Weigh Down the Holding

IYH’s #2 top-weighted holding is Johnson & Johnson at 8.3% weight. The 140-year-old company is another mediocre top holding due to its low growth. While JNJ’s oncology and cardiovascular pharmaceuticals were primary drivers of sales, other segments such as immunology and infectious diseases saw negative growth this past year.

These recent results are also consistent with its historic revenue growth. Since 2016, the healthcare company has seen only a 3% revenue CAGR. Looking forward, the company’s 2.54% dividend yield and 49% payout ratio will limit significant growth. This is because significant dividend payouts limit the research and development necessary for new products. While JNJ’s forward P/E is more reasonably priced than LLY at 18.9, its valuation remains elevated compared to its recent average. Therefore, JNJ represents the second top holding that will prevent significant capital appreciation for IYH looking forward.

AbbVie: New Products Alone Will Not Substantially Boost Profits

The third factor to consider is AbbVie, IYH’s #3 holding, at 6.7% weight. Humira, which predominantly treats inflammation, was once AbbVie’s flagship product. However, due to patent expiration, competitors, and AbbVie’s focus on other products, product sales for Humira declined roughly 50% at the start of 2025. One forecast previously made was that newer products Skyrizi and Rinvoq, which also treat inflammatory conditions, would more than make up for any loss in Humira sales through the end of the decade. While this forecast has been true so far, AbbVie struggles to see significant profitability with a net income margin of only 4%.

Moving forward, AbbVie does have several products under development. However, each of these products are still under FDA review. Given that only about 14% of pharmaceutical products fully pass FDA review, investors should be cautious in banking on AbbVie’s products under development. Without any new approved products, the company will see muted growth consistent with its YoY revenue growth, which has been less than 10%. Combining this outlook with its current valuation, including a forward P/E 57% higher than its own 5-year average, ABBV represents another unimpressive holding for IYH.

Current Valuation and 2026 Outlook

Despite high returns from LLY, JNJ, and ABBV this past year, the fund has underperformed the broader U.S. market, as measured by the S&P 500 Index. Only BBH has outperformed other compared funds, driven by Amgen (AMGN) and Gilead Sciences (GILD).

As a result of the stretched price of its top holdings, IYH has the most unattractive overall valuation compared to peer funds, as measured by P/E and P/B ratios. Given the outlook factors discussed previously and IYH’s overall valuation, I expect the fund to see subpar returns in 2026.

Valuation Metrics for IYH and Peer Healthcare Funds

IYH

BBH

IHF

IXJ

P/E ratio

33.10

26.30

19.29

31.69

P/B ratio

5.45

4.83

2.34

4.58

Source: Compiled by Author from Multiple Sources, 10 Jan 26

Risks to Investors

IYH is highly concentrated in pharmaceutical companies that rely on profits from selling their treatments. However, a key factor for these companies is the skyrocketing costs of drug development. With the average direct research and development cost of a new drug reaching $369 million, pharmaceutical companies must continuously increase sales prices. This translates to a high per capita spending in the United States, which is even higher than all other developed countries.

Rising costs translate to higher costs for patients, which ultimately puts pressure on insurance companies. These higher costs also drive government regulation, to include actions by the current U.S. presidential administration. As a result, pharmaceutical companies will face regulatory pressure despite the exponentially increasing cost to develop new products. This environment represents a significant risk factor for IYH's top holdings.

Concluding Summary

IYH has historically outperformed many peer healthcare ETFs. However, given the outlook of its top holdings, which represent a significant amount of the fund’s weight, IYH has a weaker overall forecast. Additionally, the fund has a relatively high valuation due to the stretched price of its top-weighted companies.

data.title: APi Group: An Interesting Safe Player
data.description:
Shares of APi Group (APG) have been on a tear in 2025, having seen incredible momentum driven by recognition of past performances, an acceleration of organic growth rates, as well as ambitions for 2028.

This makes me pleased that I have gotten to learn this business. While valuations look reasonable here, it is massive momentum that makes me cautious to get involved here just yet, but also very keen to learn more and continue coverage in 2026 in the hope of more interesting entry points.

About the Business

APi Group is a business service provider of fire, life safety, electronics security, elevator, escalator, and specialty services. The company operates in generally fragmented markets, which are large and growing. Simple execution and M&A efforts make the company target above-average growth, standing at the basis of shareholder value creation. The company owns and operates brands like APi, Viking, Chubb, and Saval, among many others.

By now, the business generates about $7 billion in sales, two-thirds from so-called safety services, with the remaining sales generated from specialty services. This key segment even has an outsized EBITDA contribution, responsible for about three-quarters of EBITDA. The company benefits from predictable cash flow, targeting 60% of revenues seen from inspection, service, and monitoring efforts not too long from here.

During the 2010s, the company has seen sales increase from $1.5 billion to $4.0 billion as a mid-teens compounded annual growth rate is split half-and-half between organic and M&A efforts. After an initial plunge around the time of the pandemic, the business got a major impetus after the Chubb acquisition in 2022, setting the business up to advance sales to about $7 billion by now.

The company targets 2028 revenues at $10 billion, or more, seeing margin expansion as well, with EBITDA margins seen up to 16%. Achieving such targets allows for great potential in the business and shares. This should come on top of a solid track record already, as a mere $7 stock in 2017 has made gradual inroads to levels in the low twenties early in 2025. Following a spectacular run higher, shares now trade at all-time highs around $42 per share.

Valuation Perspective

In February of last year, APi reported somewhat stable and disappointing 2024 results, with reported sales up just over a percent and organic sales down nearly a percent, as revenues just topped $7.0 billion. The core safety service segment had a decent year, with revenues growing in a solid fashion to $5.2 billion, with segment margins exceeding 15% of sales. This is complemented by declining sales at specialty services, whose segment margins were stuck at 11% and change.

Overall EBITDA margins rose less than 140 basis points to 12.7%, with EBITDA reported at $893 million. Adjusted earnings came in at $514 million, equal to $1.84 per share; this number was up 16% on the year before. This number stood in sharp contrast compared to GAAP losses reported, largely due to conversion of preferred Series B stock and some other items.

Net debt was reported at $2.2 billion, a substantial amount, but manageable given the growing EBITDA performance, certainly as the preferred stock was eliminated. With shares trading in the low twenties, based on adjusted earnings, the metrics look quite reasonable, with shares trading at a low-teens earnings multiple.

This certainly was the case, as the company guided for 2025 sales at $7.4 billion, plus or minus $100 million. EBITDA was seen at approximately $995 million, plus or minus $25 million, also aided by a full year of contribution from previous acquisitions.

2025 - On Fire

APi started 2025 with a convincing 7% increase in first-quarter sales, as organic growth accelerated to nearly 2%. Following this momentum, the company hiked the full-year guidance, hiking the sales guidance by $100 million and the EBITDA guidance by another $15 million.

Later in the spring, the company announced its 2028 goals, which included the $10 billion revenue target and 16% EBITDA margin target. Second quarter momentum was strong, certainly given sluggish real estate markets, with reported revenues up 15% and organic growth up more than 8%. This means that the full-year sales guidance was hiked by another quarter of a billion to a midpoint of $7.75 billion, although the hike in the EBITDA guidance was much less pronounced, to a midpoint of $1.025 billion.

Third-quarter sales rose as much as 14%, with organic growth reported near 10%. The sales guidance had been hiked by another $125 million to a midpoint of $7.875 billion, although that EBITDA is only seen at $1.03 billion. Strong momentum is mostly showing up in sales rather than EBITDA, suggesting that pricing/tariffs could be more of a driver than organic volume increases.

Net debt is now reported at $2.2 billion, and with EBITDA surpassing the billion-dollar mark, leverage ratios are seen in the low 2s. A modest pace of deleveraging came in part because of modest buybacks and some smaller M&A deals executed upon (so far) in 2025. With a diluted share count of 429 million shares now trading at $42, the market value of the company stands at $18.0 billion, for just over a $20 billion enterprise valuation.

This values the business at around 2.5 times sales, around 20 times EBITDA. Year-to-date adjusted earnings are up $0.16 to $1.04 per share, as the fourth quarter in this business is strong. Given that earnings might top $2 per share, multiples look rather reasonable on that front, certainly given the strong organic growth rates, driven by execution and culture.

A Bolt-On Deal to End 2025

Towards year-end 2025, APi Group reached an agreement to acquire CertaSite, an inspection-first provider of fire and life safety services in the Midwest region. The deal is set to boost annual sales by $90 million, adding over a percent to the near $8 billion revenue base of APi.

Furthermore, the company guided that both sales and EBITDA guidance for 2025 would surpass the midpoint of the previously issued guidance, and moreover, that net leverage is seen below 2 times.

And Now?

What is clear is that shares of APi Group have seen a significant re-rating over the past year. Trading in the low twenties last year, shares commanded a low-double-digit earnings multiple, as shares nearly doubled in the year following.

This came as flattish organic growth accelerated during the year, in tougher conditions, all strong achievements. This has driven a re-rating during the year, yet with shares nearly doubling, the re-rating seems very substantial and perhaps too much in my view.

Frankly, a 20 times earnings multiple looks reasonable given the organic growth that comes near 10%, yet this comes amidst adjusted earnings as well as very low valuations from the get-go. This makes me a bit cautious here, but what seems to be the case is that the business is a strong performer here. While current multiples look reasonable, I fail to see sufficient reason to chase the shares here, although I am very keen to keep up coverage here to learn more about APi in 2026.

data.title: How Much Longer Can We Avoid Thinking About The Long Term? - Weekly Blog #923
data.description:
First Week 2026

Using the performance of equity mutual funds, it was a great week with average gains of more than +2%. If repeated for each week of the year, it would produce returns of over 100%. Even the value of the US dollar rose a bit during the week.

That was the delusional news! What’s even worse, the average commodity fund invested in gold and other precious metals rose +4.02%. Funds owning stocks of gold and other precious metal mining companies gained +5.32% on average through Thursday.

The latter can suffer mining risks, labor strikes, and raised taxes. Historically, gold has been a hedge against the value of a currency, particularly the US dollar. There is also a small industrial market for gold in the electronics market, which might be in the region of $1,000 an ounce.

How much demand for gold jewelry is really demand for a convenient way to pass on its monetary value? I don’t know. Part of the demand for gold as a use in the crypto world is not known by me. All told, I suspect over half of the value of gold is as a substitute for the US dollar.

What Is The Value Of The US dollar?

Something is worth what someone is willing to pay for it. Currently, it appears to be about $0.99 , up from $0.96 . However, the critical question is its worth in the future. That appears to be what someone is willing to pay for it, delivered today or on a specific date and quantity in the future.

According to a paper prepared by the National Bureau of Economic Research twenty five years ago, people believed the US fiscal budget looking forward 10 years would be $5.9 billion, with all public debt paid off by 2006.

The readers of the One Big Beautiful Bill Act now project a 2054 debt to GDP ratio of 199%, incorporating temporary provisions. Net interest payments would rise to 6.3% from 3.2% today.

(I don’t know how to impact these numbers with the increase in gambling. In the first 11 months of 2025, total sports gambling in New Jersey was $67 billion. The rise of non-securities-backed gambling, particularly among the young, appears to be on the rise.)

Why Should We Care?

Even with the increase in retail securities markets investing, institutional investors set the prices of fixed income securities and many large-cap stocks. Most money invested through 401(k) and similar retirement accounts is invested in mutual funds or SMAs.

Insurance companies, endowments, and other institutional investors may increase their investment in foreign securities, which will impact domestic stock prices. Both domestic and foreign-controlled investors may shift some of their investment focus if the dollar becomes weaker.

Leaders Increasingly Think Globally

Foreign leaders have increasingly thought globally in determining their strategies. Our main adversaries, China, Russia, and North Korea are strategists, whereas the US tends to view the world as tacticians through domestic glasses and the next election time scale. Luckily, many of our domestic commercial leaders are increasingly thinking strategically.

Strategies Going Forward

Going forward, we should recognize that the world is changing at a rapid rate, and we need to change with it. Old rules and strategies will change. We must be careful.

Please share your thoughts.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: TIC Solutions: Valuation Not Cheap Enough
data.description:
Investment summary

My recommendation for TIC Solutions (TIC) is a hold rating. I acknowledge it has a resilient demand profile, growing exposure to secular data center and infrastructure markets, and a strong technical moat. However, valuation is not cheap, and for that reason, I am not giving a buy rating.

Business Overview

TIC was formed through the merger of Acuren and NV5 Global in August 2025. It provides testing, inspection, engineering, certification, and compliance services for critical infrastructure across North America. Some of the assets it covers include pipelines, utilities, industrial facilities, and data centers. The main objective is to help owners meet ongoing safety, regulatory, and operational requirements through recurring inspection and compliance work.

Strong and visible growth

The best part about TIC's equity story is that most of its revenue is non-discretionary, driven by regulations, insurance requirements, and safety standards. Customers can cut or delay many expenses during a macro downturn, but they cannot skip mandatory inspections, as they would risk fines or even shutdowns. As such, the costs of inspections are little relative to the cost of failure. Flip it around, this means TIC has very strong pricing power.

What makes it even better is that TIC is also serving areas with strong secular growth, such as data centers and power grid infrastructure. For context, TIC's legacy business in the oil and gas sector is volatile because spending in that sector often swings with commodity prices. When oil prices fall, producers and refiners typically cut discretionary capital spending and delay non-urgent projects, which can reduce inspection and engineering work. Data centers and power grid infrastructure are different. Demand is driven by the need for electricity and computing, not oil prices.

In the current era of the AI investment supercycle, large data center operators and utility companies have to build and maintain their assets even in a macro downturn because any power interruption is costly (to themselves and to their underlying customers because it means a shutdown of the data center and, very likely, the operations that depend on it). Demand is evidently strong here, as we can see in Q3 2025, when data center revenue doubled y/y. As growth becomes increasingly driven by these higher-value infrastructure markets, I believe the market will begin to value TIC more like a critical technology infrastructure service provider (should yield a higher multiple).

Moat

Now, for TIC's biggest moat, I believe it is the underlying technical expertise. In TIC's scope of work, in the Inspection and Mitigation segment, there is this thing called "call-out" jobs, which are basically jobs that require teams to respond quickly to assess or fix serious issues. Since this work is specialized and urgent, you would need to deploy a skilled technician on-site to make sure it's fixed. This is not your typical job where the technician is sent to check and fix issues in a nice office setting. For example, TIC has technicians certified to hang from the side of a tall structure to perform ultrasonic testing on a weld (using sound waves to detect hidden cracks). Often in refineries, TIC performs on-stream inspections on critical pipes such as hydrocarbon feed lines or reactor outlet piping, which carry extremely hot, pressurized materials and cannot be easily shut down. Hence, instead of stopping operations and losing millions in production, TIC has to inspect the pipes while the plant remains online (think about how complex and dangerous this is).

This kind of work cannot be done by someone that is non-experienced and cannot be automated. In this sense, entry barriers are high because clients require strict certifications and strong safety records before a vendor can even bid. Once TIC proves it can solve problems safely, customers tend to stick with them rather than switch to a cheaper, untested provider. This in turn gives TIC pricing power.

Synergies

The last thing to talk about is the synergies from the merger. In my view, the most important synergy here is the design-to-maintain feedback loop. This is far more powerful than cost cutting (consolidating back-office functions, optimizing insurance spending, and streamlining fleet management) because it locks in the customer for the entire asset lifecycle (hence, preventing competitors from entering). For example, before the merger, NV5 is hired to commission and certify critical systems, especially cooling. To put things in perspective, this is the part of the job scope where the risk is highest, and customers need to trust NV5 enough to engage them. In the past, NV5 would leave after commissioning. The client would later run a separate bid for a firm to do the inspection, and this opens up room for competition to enter (i.e., the potential for a price war goes up).

After the merger, NV5 includes TIC's maintenance work in the original contract. That means TIC is locked in from day one since it does the inspection work. Overall margins should go up since there is no cost to win the customer, pricing pressure is also lower because maintenance is part of a much larger project (TIC can justify a higher price), and the work is faster because TIC already knows the system. All in all, I believe this synergy will create a strong moat that subscale players are unable to copy.

Valuation

Fundamentally, TIC is great, but valuation-wise, it isn't. Even if we use management growth guidance (midpoint of the 3-5% range) and an adj. EBITDA margin of 16% (for FY26) and assume that TIC trades at 10x forward adj. EBITDA, I don't see any attractive upside. I used 10x, as this is the low end of where peers trade at. Peers include SGS SA, Bureau Veritas, Intertek, and Transcat, which have a growth range of 5-7%, an EBITDA margin >16% on average, and trade at 10x forward adj. EBITDA on average. Hence, when I compare TIC against peers, I'd say the highest multiple it could trade is about 10x.

Conclusion

My view for TIC is a hold rating. I like the fundamental aspects of TIC, where it has a non-discretionary revenue base that provides downside protection and has good exposure to verticals with strong secular growth and technical barriers to entry. However, valuation is not cheap, in my view. Even using management's guidance and a reasonable multiple, I don't see attractive upside.
